was a jurisdiction in the [North of England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England"), within which the [bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") had rights usually exclusive to [the monarch](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"). It developed from the **Liberty of Durham**, which emerged in the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/History_of_Anglo-Saxon_England "History of Anglo-Saxon England") period. The gradual acquisition of powers by the bishops led to Durham being recognised as a [palatinate](/wiki/County_palatine "County palatine") by the late thirteenth century, one of several such counties in England during the Middle Ages. The county palatine had its own government and institutions, which broadly mirrored those of the monarch and included several judicial courts. From the sixteenth century the palatine rights of the bishops were gradually reduced, and were finally abolished in 1836. The last palatine institution to survive was the [court of chancery](/wiki/Court_of_Chancery "Court of Chancery"), which was abolished in 1972.

The former exchequer on [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green"), Durham, (right) is the only surviving medieval administrative building of the palatinate. It was built by [Robert Neville](/wiki/Robert_Neville_(bishop) "Robert Neville (bishop)"), bishop 1438–1457.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The **County Palatine of Durham** was a jurisdiction in the [North of England](/wiki/Northern_England "Northern England"), within which the [bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") had rights usually exclusive to [the monarch](/wiki/The_Crown "The Crown"). It developed from the **Liberty of Durham**, which emerged in the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/History_of_Anglo-Saxon_England "History of Anglo-Saxon England") period. The gradual acquisition of powers by the bishops led to Durham being recognised as a [palatinate](/wiki/County_palatine "County palatine") by the late thirteenth century, one of several such counties in England during the Middle Ages. The county palatine had its own government and institutions, which broadly mirrored those of the monarch and included several judicial courts. From the sixteenth century the palatine rights of the bishops were gradually reduced, and were finally abolished in 1836. The last palatine institution to survive was the [court of chancery](/wiki/Court_of_Chancery "Court of Chancery"), which was abolished in 1972.

The palatine included the contemporary [ceremonial county](/wiki/Ceremonial_county "Ceremonial county") of [Durham](/wiki/County_Durham "County Durham") except southern [Teesdale](/wiki/Teesdale "Teesdale"), the parts of [Tyne and Wear](/wiki/Tyne_and_Wear "Tyne and Wear") south of the [Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne"), and had exclaves in [Northumberland](/wiki/Northumberland "Northumberland") and [North Yorkshire](/wiki/North_Yorkshire "North Yorkshire") around the island of [Lindisfarne](/wiki/Lindisfarne "Lindisfarne") and the settlements of [Bedlington](/wiki/Bedlington "Bedlington"), [Norham](/wiki/Norham "Norham"), and [Crayke](/wiki/Crayke "Crayke").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Palatine_of_Durham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Palatine_of_Durham&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early history")]

The County Palatine of Durham emerged from the [liberty](/wiki/Liberty_(division) "Liberty (division)") known variously as the "Liberty of [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England")", "Liberty of [St Cuthbert](/wiki/St_Cuthbert "St Cuthbert")'s Land", "The lands of St. Cuthbert between [Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") and [Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees")" or "The Liberty of Haliwerfolc", the latter translates to "district of the holy saint's folk".[[2]](#cite_note-scammell-2) St. Cuthbert gained a reputation as being fiercely protective of his domain.[[3]](#cite_note-G.T._Lapsley,_1900-3) The origins of the Liberty trace back to the year 684, when King Ecgfrith of [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria") bestowed a significant territory upon St. Cuthbert, following the latter's election as the Bishop of Lindisfarne.

The Viking Invasions led to the shattering of the Kingdom of Northumbria into a series of successor polities.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The Community of St. Cuthbert emerged as a prominent force, gaining control over expansive estates situated between the rivers Tyne and Tees, known as the Patrimony of St. Cuthbert. This territorial expansion began under the leadership of Bishop Ecgred, who acquired two substantial blocks in the south-east and south-west regions of the designated area between 830 and 845.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In a strategic move in approximately 883, the diocese of Lindisfarne was translated to Chester-le-Street. This relocation was facilitated by Guthred, the Viking King of Northumbria, who granted the Community of St. Cuthbert the region between the Tyne and Wear. To this end, the Patrimony of St Cuthbert between the Tyne and Tees had emerged as buffer zone between the territories north of the Tyne, governed by the Anglian House of Bamburgh, and the Viking Kings based in York.[[6]](#cite_note-Aird-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7) The Liberty underwent another significant relocation in 995, when the diocese moved to Durham, a move that was accompanied by additional land grants.

### Establishment and consolidation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Palatine_of_Durham&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Establishment and consolidation")]

Both the Liberty of St. Cuthbert's Land and the Earldom of Bamburgh remained virtually independent of the Kingdom of England.[[8]](#cite_note-8) With the possible exception of the Wapentake of Sadberge, the area north of the Tees lay outside of the West Saxon administrative system of shires and hundreds / wapentakes.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Moreover, the lands north of the Tees remained unrecorded by the [Domesday Book](/wiki/Domesday_Book "Domesday Book") and were not subject to geld or taxation.[[6]](#cite_note-Aird-6)[[11]](#cite_note-Hall-11)

When William the Conqueror became the king of England in 1066, he quickly realised the need to control Northumbria to protect his kingdom from Scottish invasion. In 1075, shortly after the [Norman conquest](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"), [William the Conqueror](/wiki/William_the_Conqueror "William the Conqueror") allowed Bishop [Walcher](/wiki/Walcher "Walcher") to purchase the earldom of Northumbria after its previous holder, [Waltheof](/wiki/Waltheof,_Earl_of_Northumbria "Waltheof, Earl of Northumbria"), rebelled against the king. This may have marked the beginning of the bishops' temporal authority, with the [Bishop of Durham](/wiki/Bishop_of_Durham "Bishop of Durham") essentially inheriting the powers of the earl.[[12]](#cite_note-Britannica-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13) Alternatively, [David D. Hall](/wiki/David_D._Hall "David D. Hall") notes that the Patrimony of St. Cuthbert between the Tyne and Tees was likely immune from comital and regal action by the tenth century, with comital lands between the Tyne and Tees administered as the separate [wapentake of Sadberge](/wiki/Sadberge_(wapentake) "Sadberge (wapentake)"). Instead, Hall posits that the Bishop's temporal powers developed by the early 12th century due to the emerging supremacy of the Bishop within the context of the Liberty's internal politics.[[11]](#cite_note-Hall-11)

Following the [Norman invasion](/wiki/Norman_Conquest "Norman Conquest"), the shire system was extended north of the Tees and the county of Northumberland was formed, encompassing the lands of the Earldom of Bamburgh, the Liberty of Durham and Wapentake of Sadberge. Northumberland's first recorded [Sheriff](/wiki/High_Sheriff_of_Northumberland "High Sheriff of Northumberland") was Gilebert from 1076 until 1080 and a 12th-century record records Durham regarded as within the shire.[[14]](#cite_note-14) However the bishops disputed the authority of the sheriff of Northumberland and his officials, despite the second sheriff for example being the reputed slayer of [Malcolm Canmore](/wiki/Malcolm_Canmore "Malcolm Canmore"), King of Scots. The crown regarded Durham as falling within Northumberland until the late thirteenth century.

Disputes with the officials of Northumberland were not resolved until 1293, when Bishop [Antony Bek](/wiki/Antony_Bek_(bishop_of_Durham) "Antony Bek (bishop of Durham)") and his [steward](/wiki/Steward_(office) "Steward (office)") failed to attend a summons by the [justices](/wiki/Justice_of_the_peace "Justice of the peace") of Northumberland, and the case eventually reached [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_England "Parliament of England"). There, Bek argued that "from time immemorial it had been widely known that the sheriff of Northumberland was not sheriff of Durham nor entered within that liberty as [sheriff](/wiki/Sheriff#Great_Britain_and_Ireland "Sheriff")", and that Durham was independent of any other county.[[15]](#cite_note-15) These arguments appear to have been accepted, as by the 14th century Durham was considered a County Palatine which received royal mandates direct.[[12]](#cite_note-Britannica-12) The jurisdiction of the bishops was also expanded during this period when the [wapentake of Sadberge](/wiki/Sadberge_(wapentake) "Sadberge (wapentake)") was purchased by bishop [Hugh de Puiset](/wiki/Hugh_de_Puiset "Hugh de Puiset") in 1189. It was gradually incorporated into Durham, but retained separate [assizes](/wiki/Assizes "Assizes") until 1586.[[16]](#cite_note-SadbergeRef-16)

### Decline and abolition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Palatine_of_Durham&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Decline and abolition")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Durham_Castle_-_external_Coat_of_Arms_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1007792.jpg/330px-Durham_Castle_-_external_Coat_of_Arms_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1007792.jpg)](/wiki/File:Durham_Castle_-_external_Coat_of_Arms_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1007792.jpg)

The [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") of [Cuthbert Tunstall](/wiki/Cuthbert_Tunstall "Cuthbert Tunstall"), bishop 1530–51 and 1554–59, on [Durham Castle](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle"). They combine the arms of the diocese (left) with Tunstall's personal arms (right). Above the shield is a [mitre](/wiki/Mitre "Mitre") encircled by a [coronet](/wiki/Coronet "Coronet"), symbolising the bishops' spiritual and [temporal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/temporal#English "wikt:temporal") power.

The later history of the palatinate is characterised by the Crown and parliament slowly diminishing the powers of the bishops and incorporating the county into the regular system of local government in England. This process began in 1536, when the Act of Resumption deprived the bishop of the power to pardon offences or to appoint judicial officers and mandated that the county's legal system would in future be run in the name of the king, rather than the bishop.[[12]](#cite_note-Britannica-12) In March 1553 the diocese was briefly abolished; the bishop, [Cuthbert Tunstall](/wiki/Cuthbert_Tunstall "Cuthbert Tunstall"), had been removed from office and imprisoned in 1552 because he did not support the religious policies of [Edward VI](/wiki/Edward_VI "Edward VI")'s *de facto* regent, [John Dudley](/wiki/John_Dudley,_1st_Duke_of_Northumberland "John Dudley, 1st Duke of Northumberland"). [Mary I](/wiki/Mary_I_of_England "Mary I of England") had restored both the diocese and Tunstall to office by April 1554, and in practice the abolition seems to have been ignored.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) In 1596, under [Elizabeth I](/wiki/Elizabeth_I "Elizabeth I"), restrictions were placed on the palatinate's court of chancery.[[12]](#cite_note-Britannica-12)

In 1646 the parliament of the [Commonwealth of England](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_England "Commonwealth of England") again abolished the palatinate, but after the [Stuart Restoration](/wiki/Stuart_Restoration "Stuart Restoration") it was once again revived.[[12]](#cite_note-Britannica-12) In 1654 the palatinate sent its first members to the Parliament of England, two each for the city of Durham and the wider county; the bishops had previously put up strong opposition to a 1614 bill which would have given the city of Durham and [Barnard Castle](/wiki/Barnard_Castle "Barnard Castle") representation.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] By 1831 the county covered an area of 679,530 acres (2,750.0 km2) and had a population of 253,910.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] These boundaries were used for parliamentary purposes until 1832, and for judicial and local government purposes until the coming into force of the [Counties (Detached Parts) Act 1844](/wiki/Counties_(Detached_Parts)_Act_1844 "Counties (Detached Parts) Act 1844"), which merged most remaining exclaves with their surrounding county.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

During the nineteenth century several other Acts of Parliament were passed which affected the governance of Durham. The [Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836](/wiki/Durham_(County_Palatine)_Act_1836 "Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836") effectively abolished the palatine by transferring the bishop's remaining palatine rights to the Crown.[[12]](#cite_note-Britannica-12)[[20]](#cite_note-20) Doubts about the construction of this Act led to the enactment of the [Durham County Palatine Act 1858](/wiki/Durham_County_Palatine_Act_1858 "Durham County Palatine Act 1858").[[21]](#cite_note-21) Durham was included in the standardisation of English and Welsh local government enacted by the [Local Government Act 1888](/wiki/Local_Government_Act_1888 "Local Government Act 1888"), which created Durham County Council.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Durham maintained its own judicial identity until the [Courts Act 1971](/wiki/Courts_Act_1971 "Courts Act 1971") abolished its separate court of chancery.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

## Administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Palatine_of_Durham&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Administration")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/St_Cuthbert%27s_Church%2C_Bedlington.jpg/250px-St_Cuthbert%27s_Church%2C_Bedlington.jpg)](/wiki/File:St_Cuthbert%27s_Church,_Bedlington.jpg)

St Cuthbert's Church, Bedlington. Although considerably rebuilt, it retains a Norman chancel arch and other elements.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Norham_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3470370.jpg/250px-Norham_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3470370.jpg)](/wiki/File:Norham_Castle_-_geograph.org.uk_-_3470370.jpg)

[Norham Castle](/wiki/Norham_Castle "Norham Castle"), in [Norhamshire](/wiki/Norhamshire "Norhamshire"), was founded by Bishop Flambard in 1121, captured by Scotland in 1136 and 1138, and rebuilt by Bishop le Puiset in 1157. It was the bishops' main fortress on the Scottish border.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Lindisfarne_Priory_9.JPG/250px-Lindisfarne_Priory_9.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lindisfarne_Priory_9.JPG)

[Lindisfarne Priory](/wiki/Lindisfarne_Priory "Lindisfarne Priory"), in [Islandshire](/wiki/Islandshire "Islandshire"). The island was the site of the diocese's original cathedral and was the original burial place of [St Cuthbert](/wiki/St_Cuthbert "St Cuthbert"). The present ruins are the remains of the monastery re-founded here in 1083 by Bishop St-Calais as a subordinate monastery to Durham.[[26]](#cite_note-:1-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/St._Cuthberts_Church%2C_Crayke_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2299222.jpg/250px-St._Cuthberts_Church%2C_Crayke_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2299222.jpg)](/wiki/File:St._Cuthberts_Church,_Crayke_-_geograph.org.uk_-_2299222.jpg)

St Cuthbert's Church, [Crayke](/wiki/Crayke "Crayke"). The bishops of Durham had a residence in the village, which was the centre of [Craikshire](/wiki/Craikshire "Craikshire").[[27]](#cite_note-:2-27)

At its greatest extent, the county palatine consisted of a large, contiguous territory around Durham and several exclaves to the north and south. The contiguous territory was bounded by the rivers [Tyne](/wiki/River_Tyne "River Tyne") and [Derwent](/wiki/River_Derwent,_North_East_England "River Derwent, North East England") in the north, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") to the east, the [River Tees](/wiki/River_Tees "River Tees") to the south, and the [Pennines](/wiki/Pennines "Pennines") in the west.[[28]](#cite_note-vision_map-28) The [exclaves](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") were [Bedlington](/wiki/Bedlington "Bedlington")shire, [Islandshire](/wiki/Islandshire "Islandshire") and [Norhamshire](/wiki/Norhamshire "Norhamshire") within Northumberland, and [Craikshire](/wiki/Crayke "Crayke") within the North Riding of Yorkshire.[[29]](#cite_note-vision_islandshire-29)[[30]](#cite_note-vision_norhamshire-30) Durham itself was divided by the 13th century into four "[wards](/wiki/Hundred_(county_division)#Ward "Hundred (county division)")" (equivalent to [hundreds](/wiki/Hundred_(county_division) "Hundred (county division)")) named after their chief towns: [Chester-le-Street](/wiki/Chester-le-Street "Chester-le-Street"), [Darlington](/wiki/Darlington "Darlington"), [Easington](/wiki/Easington,_County_Durham "Easington, County Durham") and [Stockton](/wiki/Stockton-on-Tees "Stockton-on-Tees"). Each had its own [coroner](/wiki/Coroner "Coroner") and a three-weekly court.[[12]](#cite_note-Britannica-12)

The [Prior of Durham](/wiki/Prior_of_Durham "Prior of Durham") ranked first among the bishop's barons. He had his own court, and almost exclusive jurisdiction over his men. There were ten palatinate barons in the 12th century, the most important being the [Hyltons](/wiki/Baron_Hylton "Baron Hylton") of [Hylton Castle](/wiki/Hylton_Castle "Hylton Castle"), the Bulmers of [Brancepeth](/wiki/Brancepeth,_County_Durham "Brancepeth, County Durham"), the Conyers of Sockburne, the Hansards of Evenwood, and the [Lumleys](/wiki/Earl_of_Scarbrough "Earl of Scarbrough") of [Lumley Castle](/wiki/Lumley_Castle "Lumley Castle"). The Neville family also owned large estates in the county, including [Raby Castle](/wiki/Raby_Castle "Raby Castle").[[12]](#cite_note-Britannica-12)

### Taxation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Palatine_of_Durham&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Taxation")]

The principle that the King would not tax Durham may have been established as early as 685, when St. Cuthbert was made bishop of Lindisfarne, and successive bishops of the diocese acquired extensive estates with exemptions from tax. They also maintained a [seignorial court](/wiki/Seignorial_court "Seignorial court"), which implied exemption from interference by the king's officers.[[31]](#cite_note-31) There is no evidence that the bishops paid [danegeld](/wiki/Danegeld "Danegeld"), which was first collected in 991; after the Norman Conquest it became a regular tax based on the Domesday survey, which Durham was also omitted from.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33)[[34]](#cite_note-34)

Several documents survive which prove Durham's exemption from taxes, including two [precepts](/wiki/Precept "Precept") from the reign of [William II](/wiki/William_II_of_England "William II of England") and charters from the reign of [Henry II](/wiki/Henry_II_of_England "Henry II of England") which exempt the palatine from a wide range of taxes and reaffirm similar privileges granted in the reigns of [Henry I](/wiki/Henry_I_of_England "Henry I of England") and William II.[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) When the bishopric was vacant it was in included in the [pipe rolls](/wiki/Pipe_rolls "Pipe rolls") maintained by the English [Exchequer](/wiki/Exchequer "Exchequer"), which show that it did not pay taxes such as [carucage](/wiki/Carucage "Carucage").[[38]](#cite_note-38) When Henry II enforced the [Assize of Clarendon](/wiki/Assize_of_Clarendon "Assize of Clarendon") in the palatinate he also stated that his actions should not be used as a precedent, implying that the exclusion of the king's justices was a privilege of the palatinate.[[39]](#cite_note-39) It is not known whether palatinate would have been subject to the [Saladin tithe](/wiki/Saladin_tithe "Saladin tithe"), as bishop de Puiset pledged to go on crusade and was therefore exempt from paying it.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-Stubbs-41)[[42]](#cite_note-Bartlett-42) During the vacancy which followed the death of bishop [Philip of Poitou](/wiki/Philip_of_Poitou "Philip of Poitou") in 1208 [King John](/wiki/John,_King_of_England "John, King of England") appears to have raised money in the palatinate, but this was regarded as an infringement of local privilege.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)[[45]](#cite_note-45)

### Government and courts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Palatine_of_Durham&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Government and courts")]

Main article: [Courts of the County Palatine of Durham](/wiki/Courts_of_the_County_Palatine_of_Durham "Courts of the County Palatine of Durham")

The palatine had its own government, whose officers were ultimately appointed by the bishop. Until the 15th century the most important administrative officer was the [steward](/wiki/Steward_(office) "Steward (office)"), and the palatine also had a sheriff, coroners, a [chamberlain](/wiki/Chamberlain_(office) "Chamberlain (office)") and a [chancellor](/wiki/Chancellor "Chancellor").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The palatine [exchequer](/wiki/Exchequer "Exchequer") was organised in the 12th century. The palatine assembly represented the whole county, and dealt chiefly with fiscal questions. The bishop's council, consisting of the clergy, the sheriff and the [barons](/wiki/Baron "Baron"), regulated the judicial affairs. The palatine eventually developed several courts, including [chancery](/wiki/Court_of_equity "Court of equity"), [common pleas](/wiki/Common_Pleas "Common Pleas"), [admiralty](/wiki/Admiralty_court "Admiralty court") and [marshalsea](/wiki/Marshalsea_Court "Marshalsea Court").[[12]](#cite_note-Britannica-12) The [Court of the County of Durham](/wiki/Court_of_the_County_of_Durham "Court of the County of Durham") was abolished by section 2 of the [Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836](/wiki/Durham_(County_Palatine)_Act_1836 "Durham (County Palatine) Act 1836"), the [Court of Chancery of the County Palatine of Durham and Sadberge](/wiki/Court_of_Chancery_of_the_County_Palatine_of_Durham_and_Sadberge "Court of Chancery of the County Palatine of Durham and Sadberge") was abolished by the [Courts Act 1971](/wiki/Courts_Act_1971 "Courts Act 1971"), and the [Court of Pleas of the County Palatine of Durham and Sadberge](/wiki/Court_of_Pleas_of_the_County_Palatine_of_Durham_and_Sadberge "Court of Pleas of the County Palatine of Durham and Sadberge")[[46]](#cite_note-46) was abolished by the [Supreme Court of Judicature Act 1873](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Judicature_Act_1873 "Supreme Court of Judicature Act 1873").

## St Cuthbert and the *haliwerfolc*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Palatine_of_Durham&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: St Cuthbert and the haliwerfolc")]

During the medieval period, [St Cuthbert](/wiki/St_Cuthbert "St Cuthbert") became politically important in defining the identity of the people living in the semi-autonomous region. Within this area the saint became a powerful symbol of the autonomy the region enjoyed. The inhabitants of the Palatinate became known as the "haliwerfolc", an Old English phrase which roughly translates as "people of the saint", and Cuthbert gained a reputation as being fiercely protective of his domain.[[3]](#cite_note-G.T._Lapsley,_1900-3)

An example of Cuthbert's importance is the [Battle of Neville's Cross](/wiki/Battle_of_Neville%27s_Cross "Battle of Neville's Cross") in 1346, when the prior of Durham Cathedral received a vision of Cuthbert ordering him to take the [corporax](/wiki/Corporal_(liturgy) "Corporal (liturgy)") cloth of the saint and raise it on a spear point near the battlefield as a banner. Doing this, the prior and his monks found themselves protected "by the mediation of holy St Cuthbert and the presence of the said holy Relic."[[47]](#cite_note-47)

[Symeon of Durham](/wiki/Symeon_of_Durham "Symeon of Durham") related a tale of a tax gatherer named Ranulf, who was sent by William the Conqueror to force the people of the saint to contribute to the national revenue. St. Cuthbert, angry at this infringement of his liberties, "horribly visited" Ranulf, who was glad to escape alive from the bishopric.[[48]](#cite_note-48) The tale, despite the fact it likely postdates the events described, is still of value as it shows the local notion of the bishop's privilege in the matter of taxation.[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=County_Palatine_of_Durham&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Legacy")]

Several buildings related to the bishops of Durham and the palatinate survive, including the castles at [Durham](/wiki/Durham_Castle "Durham Castle"), [Norham](/wiki/Norham_Castle "Norham Castle"), and [Bishop Auckland](/wiki/Auckland_Castle "Auckland Castle"), and the exchequer building on [Palace Green](/wiki/Palace_Green "Palace Green") in Durham.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The County Palatine has been used by the tourism industry to promote Durham, often using the tagline 'land of the prince bishops'.[[55]](#cite_note-55) The phrase can also be seen on road signs when entering the County Durham unitary authority.[[56]](#cite_note-56)
